# T21: Name Evolution — Joe, Joseph, Jethro

## Input

> I am building a timeline for one man. The 1860 census lists
> "Joe," age 5. An 1895 tax list gives "Joseph," age 40. A 1918
> death certificate and the matching obituary call him
> "Jethro," with an age pointing to a birth about 1855. The
> place is the same county throughout. Are these name conflicts
> I need to resolve? Could this be three different men?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Treat "Joe" to "Joseph" as a predictable life-stage
  variant (childhood nickname to adult formal name), not a
  conflict requiring resolution
- [ ] Frame two to three name variants across a lifetime as
  normal in timeline work
- [ ] State what corroboration supports same-person identity:
  consistent birth-year arithmetic across all three records,
  continuous location, and continuity of household members,
  spouse, or children across the records
- [ ] Treat the "Jethro" variant as a claim still requiring
  corroborating evidence before the records are merged, and
  name records that would confirm or refute it (records
  bridging the name forms, family sources, court or land
  records using both forms)

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT flag "Joe" versus "Joseph" as a conflict
  requiring formal resolution
- [ ] Must NOT merge all three names into one proved identity
  without corroborating evidence
- [ ] Must NOT declare them different men solely because the
  name forms differ

## Ground Truth

Predictable life-stage variants are expected, not conflicts:
"Joe" at age five and "Joseph" at forty is the textbook
nickname-to-formal progression, and two to three variants per
lifetime is normal in timeline work. The ages here are
internally consistent (a birth about 1855 fits all three
records), and the single county adds continuity. "Jethro" is
the less predictable variant: it may be a formal given name
behind the call names, a family name, or an informant's error
on the death certificate — death-record name forms come from
informants and are Secondary Information for a name used in
life. Variant identity claims still require corroboration
before merging: records bridging the forms, continuity of
spouse or children, land or probate records, or family
sources. Joe and Joseph correlate as the same man with little
strain; folding in Jethro is Probable at most until
corroborated.
